message.content: Propose a worklist item to switch from using the `gh` CLI to use `entire` CLI instead and use trails instead of issues etc. No more github to be used by this app

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codebase-analyzer: Analyzes codebase implementation details. Call the codebase-analyzer agent when you need to find detailed information about specific components. As always, the more detailed your request prompt, the better! :) (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

- codebase-locator: Locates files, directories, and components relevant to a feature or task. Call `codebase-locator` with human language prompt describing what you're looking for. Basically a "Super Grep/Glob/LS tool" — Use it if you find yourself desiring to use one of these tools more than once. (Tools: Grep, Glob, LS)

- codebase-pattern-finder: codebase-pattern-finder is a useful subagent_type for finding similar implementations, usage examples, or existing patterns that can be modeled after. It will give you concrete code examples based on what you're looking for! It's sorta like codebase-locator, but it will not only tell you the location of files, it will also give you code details! (Tools: Grep, Glob, Read, LS)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)
- thoughts-analyzer: The research equivalent of codebase-analyzer. Use this subagent_type when wanting to deep dive on a research topic. Not commonly needed otherwise. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

- thoughts-locator: Discovers relevant documents in thoughts/ directory (We use this for all sorts of metadata storage!). This is really only relevant/needed when you're in a reseaching mood and need to figure out if we have random thoughts written down that are relevant to your current research task. Based on the name, I imagine you can guess this is the `thoughts` equivilent of `codebase-locator` (Tools: Grep, Glob, LS)

- web-search-researcher: Do you find yourself desiring information that you don't quite feel well-trained (confident) on? Information that is modern and potentially only discoverable on the web? Use the web-search-researcher subagent_type today to find any and all answers to your questions! It will research deeply to figure out and attempt to answer your questions! If you aren't immediately satisfied you can get your money back! (Not really - but you can re-run web-search-researcher with an altered prompt in the event you're not satisfied the first time) (Tools: WebSearch, WebFetch, TodoWrite, Read, Grep, Glob, LS)

## claude.ai Datadog
This is an MCP server for Datadog (a software observability platform). Consult Datadog docs if you're unsure about how something works in the platform. Docs for this MCP server can be found at https://docs.datadoghq.com/bits_ai/mcp_server/setup/ This server ships skill guides containing domain-specific guidance, workflows, and best practices that are not visible in tool names or schemas. Before invoking other tools, always perform skill discovery by running both paths in parallel: a direct load with `load_datadog_skill` using a general domain name (e.g. skill_name='datadog/traces'), and a call to `list_datadog_skills` with a fuzzy `query` based on topic keywords — load any clearly matching result from the listing along with the related skills it points to. The exception is a follow-up call in a domain whose skill you have already loaded or confirmed absent this session. If nothing clearly fits, do not load a skill — proceed with the tool descriptions. When the user asks to visualize, chart, or graph data — or when loading a data domain skill (e.g. datadog/traces, datadog/logs, datadog/metrics) — also load datadog/visualizations. Investigation results often benefit from charts.

## claude.ai Honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.
   - Alert / SLO state — for "what's bro… [truncated]

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

attachment.content:
- flash-card-creator: Create Anki-compatible flashcards from documents (PDFs, text files, markdown). Use when users ask to create flashcards, study cards, or Anki decks from uploaded documents or specific topics. Generates CSV files with basic, reversed basic, and cloze deletion card types following first principles learning progression.
- tdd: Guides Claude Code through strict Red-Green-Refactor TDD cycles. Claude writes one failing test, implements the minimum code to make it pass, then pauses and asks the user whether to refactor or move to the next red test. Use this skill whenever a user wants to build something test-first, step by step.
- testing-without-mocks: Write fast, maintainable unit tests using TDD with Nullables instead of mocks. Use when writing tests, setting up test infrastructure, or refactoring test suites.
- tiger-style-development: Comprehensive coding methodology for mission-critical software prioritizing safety, performance, and developer experience. Use when the user explicitly requests "Tiger Style" or "tigerstyle" code, or when building financial systems, databases, distributed systems, or any mission-critical code where correctness and reliability are paramount.
- create_plan: Implementation Plan
- implement_plan: Implement Plan
- research_codebase: Research Codebase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content: Auto mode lets Claude handle permission prompts automatically — Claude checks each tool call for risky actions and prompt injection before executing. Actions Claude identifies as safe are executed, while actions Claude identifies as risky are blocked and Claude may try a different approach. Ideal for long-running tasks. Sessions are slightly more expensive. Claude can make mistakes that allow harmful commands to run, it's recommended to only use in isolated environments. Shift+Tab to change mode.

content.text: `entire` doesn't show a `trail` command at the top level. Let me dig into where "trails" live and understand the actual `gh` usage in the app's runtime code (not docs).

input.command: for c in labs session activity dispatch recap; do echo "=== entire $c --help ==="; entire $c --help 2>&1 | head -30; done; echo "=== grep trail ==="; entire help 2>&1 | grep -i trail
input.description: Explore entire subcommands for trails

=== entire labs --help ===
Labs

These are newer Entire workflows we are actively refining. They are available
to try now, but details may change based on feedback.

Available experimental commands:
  entire review             Run a multi-agent review against the current branch
  entire investigate        Run a multi-agent investigation against a topic, issue, or seed doc
  entire import claude-code Import existing Claude Code transcripts as local, read-only history
  entire tokens             Analyze experimental token usage diagnostics
  entire tokens profile     Aggregate token usage across committed checkpoints
  entire org                Manage Entire organizations (create, list)
  entire project            Manage Entire projects (create, list)
  entire repo               Manage Entire repositories (create, list, get, delete)
  entire grant              Manage access grants and org membership (org, project, repo)
  entire blame              Show which lines came from Entire checkpoints
  entire why                Show why a line exists (commit, checkpoint, prompt, session)

Try:
  entire review --help
  entire investigate --help
  entire tokens --help
  entire tokens profile --help
  entire org --help
  entire project --help
  entire repo --help
  entire grant --help
  entire blame --help
  entire why --help

=== entire session --help ===
View and manage agent sessions tracked by Entire.

Commands:
  list     List all sessions across all worktrees
  info     Show detailed information for a specific session
  tokens   Show token usage and optimization recommendations
  stop     Stop one or more active sessions
  current  Show the active session for the current worktree
  attach   Attach an existing agent session
  adopt    Adopt an active session from another worktree
  resume   Switch to a branch and resume its session

Examples:
  entire session list                      List all sessions
  entire session info <session-id>         Show session details
  entire session info <session-id> --json  Output as JSON
  entire session tokens <session-id>       Show token usage
  entire session stop                      Interactive stop
  entire session current                   Active session for cwd
  entire session attach <session-id>       Attach an external session
  entire session adopt <session-id> --from ../repo  Adopt a moved session
  entire session resume <branch>           Resume from a branch

Usage:
  entire session [command]

Aliases:
  session, sessions

Available Commands:
=== entire activity --help ===
Display your activity overview, repository breakdown, and recent commits from entire.io

Usage:
  entire activity [flags]

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for activity
=== entire dispatch --help ===
Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work.

Examples:
  entire dispatch
  entire dispatch --local --all-branches
  entire dispatch --repos entireio/cli
  entire dispatch --voice neutral

Usage:
  entire dispatch [flags]

Flags:
      --all-branches    include every existing local branch (--local only; renamed or deleted branches are skipped)
  -h, --help            help for dispatch
      --local           generate via the locally-installed agent CLI instead of the Entire server
      --repos strings   cloud repo slugs, up to 5 (for example entireio/cli)
      --since string    time window (Go duration, relative time, or ISO date) (default "7d")
      --until string    window end time (defaults to now)
      --voice string    voice preset name or literal description
=== entire recap --help ===
Summarize recent checkpoint activity

Usage:
  entire recap [flags]

Flags:
      --90                   Rolling 90 days
      --agent string         Agent id to show, or all (default "all")
      --color string         Color output: auto, always, or never (default "auto")
      --day                  Today only (default)
  -h, --help                 help for recap
      --insecure-http-auth   Allow plain-HTTP auth (local dev only)
      --month                This calendar month
      --static               Print static output instead of opening the interactive recap
      --view string          Which columns to show: you, team, or both (default "both")
      --week                 Last 7 days

Generate a dispatch summarizing recent agent work.

Examples:
  entire dispatch
  entire dispatch --local --all-branches
  entire dispatch --repos entireio/cli
  entire dispatch --voice neutral

Usage:
  entire dispatch [flags]

Flags:
      --all-branches    include every existing local branch (--local only; renamed or deleted branches are skipped)
  -h, --help            help for dispatch
      --local           generate via the locally-installed agent CLI instead of the Entire server
      --repos strings   cloud repo slugs, up to 5 (for example entireio/cli)
      --since string    time window (Go duration, relative time, or ISO date) (default "7d")
      --until string    window end time (defaults to now)
      --voice string    voice preset name or literal description
=== entire recap --help ===
Summarize recent checkpoint activity

Usage:
  entire recap [flags]

Flags:
      --90                   Rolling 90 days
      --agent string         Agent id to show, or all (default "all")
      --color string         Color output: auto, always, or never (default "auto")
      --day                  Today only (default)
  -h, --help                 help for recap
      --insecure-http-auth   Allow plain-HTTP auth (local dev only)
      --month                This calendar month
      --static               Print static output instead of opening the interactive recap
      --view string          Which columns to show: you, team, or both (default "both")
      --week                 Last 7 days
=== grep trail ===

aiTitle: Migrate from gh CLI to entire CLI with trails

content.text: There's a hidden `entire trail` command. Let me get its actual interface.

input.command: echo "=== entire trail --help ==="; entire trail --help 2>&1 | head -60; echo; echo "=== entire trail list --help ==="; entire trail list --help 2>&1 | head -40
input.description: Get entire trail command interface

content.content:
=== entire trail --help ===
A trail ties together the context for a branch. Use `entire trail` to view, create, update, or watch it.

Usage:
  entire trail [flags]
  entire trail [command]

Available Commands:
  checkout    Check out a trail's branch
  create      Create a trail for the current, a new, or no branch
  delete      Delete a trail
  finding     Manage a trail's agent findings
  list        List recent trails
  resume      Resume a trail's agent session
  show        Show a trail
  update      Update trail metadata
  watch       Tail a trail's events live

Flags:
  -h, --help          help for trail
      --repo string   Target repository as forge/owner/repo (e.g. gh/acme/app) or a clone URL; defaults to the origin remote

Use "entire trail [command] --help" for more information about a command.

=== entire trail list --help ===
List recent trails

Usage:
  entire trail list [flags]

Flags:
      --author string   Filter by author login (case-insensitive); use 'me' for yourself (requires gh CLI); omit for any author
  -h, --help            help for list
      --json            Output as JSON (respects --author, --status, and --limit)
  -n, --limit int       Maximum number of trails to show (default 10)
      --status string   Filter by comma-separated status(es): draft, open, merged, closed; use 'any' for all statuses (default "open")

Global Flags:
      --repo string   Target repository as forge/owner/repo (e.g. gh/acme/app) or a clone URL; defaults to the origin remote

content.content:
=== entire trail create --help ===
Create a trail for the current, a new, or no branch

Usage:
  entire trail create [flags]

Flags:
      --base string     Base branch (defaults to detected default branch)
      --body string     Trail body
      --branch string   Branch for the trail (defaults to current branch)
      --checkout        Check out the branch after creating it
  -h, --help            help for create
      --no-branch       Create a branchless trail
      --status string   Initial status (defaults to open)
      --title string    Trail title

Global Flags:
      --repo string   Target repository as forge/owner/repo (e.g. gh/acme/app) or a clone URL; defaults to the origin remote

=== entire trail show --help ===
Show a trail.

If <trail> is omitted, shows the trail for the current branch (or --branch).
Otherwise, <trail> may be a trail number, id, or branch in the target repo.

Usage:
  entire trail show [<trail>] [flags]

Flags:
      --branch string   Show the trail for this branch instead of the current branch; cannot be combined with a trail selector
  -h, --help            help for show

Global Flags:
      --repo string   Target repository as forge/owner/repo (e.g. gh/acme/app) or a clone URL; defaults to the origin remote

=== entire trail update --help ===
Update trail metadata

Usage:
  entire trail update [flags]

Flags:
      --add-label strings      Add label(s)
      --body string            Update body
      --branch string          Branch to update trail for (defaults to current)
  -h, --help                   help for update
      --remove-label strings   Remove label(s)
      --status string          Update status
      --title string           Update title

Global Flags:
      --repo string   Target repository as forge/owner/repo (e.g. gh/acme/app) or a clone URL; defaults to the origin remote

=== entire trail finding --help ===
Manage a trail's agent-native findings.

Running 'entire trail finding' shows the finding dashboard for the current
branch's trail. Pass a trail selector (number, id, or branch) to inspect another
trail in the same repo. Use 'entire trail list --status any' when you need to
discover a trail selector first.

Usage:
  entire trail finding [<trail>] [flags]
  entire trail finding [command]

Available Commands:
  add         Create a finding on a trail
  apply       Apply a finding's unified-diff suggestion
  dismiss     Dismiss a finding
  list        List findings for a trail
  reopen      Reopen a finding
  resolve     Resolve a finding
  show        Show a finding
  update      Update a finding's metadata

Flags:
      --branch string       Resolve the trail for this branch instead of the current branch; cannot be combined with a trail selector
      --freshness string    Filter code-version freshness: current,stale,any (default "current")
  -h, --help                help for finding

input.content:
# Before

Bram is coupled to GitHub through the `gh` CLI. Everything issue-shaped
in the app shells out to `gh`, and the agent-facing conventions are
written around GitHub issues.

Runtime touchpoints (all in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` unless noted):

- **Issue routes** — `/__issues` (list), `/__issue` (view), `/__issues/search`,
  `/__issue/comment`, `/__issue/close`, `/__repo/origin`. Backed by
  `gh_issues_list`, `gh_issues_search`, `gh_issue_view`,
  `gh_issue_comment`, `gh_issue_close`, `gh_issue_cross_references`,
  `gh_issue_closed_event_actor`, `enrich_issue_activity`.
- **Close-on-commit** — `gh_commit_visible` (`gh api` commit lookup),
  `gh_commit_missing_stderr`, `gh_issue_close_with_commit`,
  `handle_issue_close_json`, `close_issue_commit_comment`,
  `parse_github_commit_url`, plus the `gh api /user` login used to
  attribute commits.
- **Issues tab UI** — `app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` binds the six
  routes above and renders the whole issue browser/search/new-issue flow.
- **Close-on-commit dialog** — `app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui`
  drives `closesIssues`, the confirm dialog, and the
  `close-issue:` / `push-before-close:` feedback protocol.
- **Allowlist** — `.claude/settings.json` permits the `/__issue*`
  loopback curl.
- **Conventions** — `CLAUDE.md` and `app/__shell/conventions.md` document
  `closesIssues`, the close-on-commit dialog, "Updating GitHub issues via
  gh", and the `gh issue`-only work carve-out. `docs/apis.md` documents
  the `/__issue*` routes.

# After

Remove all `gh`-CLI usage from Bram and replace the GitHub-issue concept
with **Entire trails** (`entire trail …`), a branch-scoped context object
with `create` / `show` / `list` / `update` / `delete` / `finding` /
`watch` subcommands. The app makes zero `gh` calls; trail work goes
through `entire trail` (which itself addresses a repo as
`--repo gh/owner/repo`, so GitHub can remain the underlying *forge*
without Bram ever invoking `gh`).

Concept mapping:

| Today (`gh`) | Replacement (`entire trail`) |
|---|---|
| `gh issue list` → `/__issues` | `entire trail list --status any --json` |
| `gh issue view N` → `/__issue` | `entire trail show <n> --json` |
| `gh issue list --search q` → `/__issues/search` | `entire trail list --json` + local filter |
| `gh issue comment` → `/__issue/comment` | `entire trail finding add` / `entire trail update` |
| `gh issue close` → `/__issue/close` | `entire trail update --status closed` (+ `finding resolve`) |
| `closesIssues` / close-on-commit dialog | `resolvesTrails` — `entire trail update --status merged/closed` after commit |
| `gh api` commit-visible verify | trail status update is local-first; drop the GitHub round-trip |

**Scope boundary:** `git` itself stays — commit and push (Commits-tab
Push button, worklist-commit route) are git operations, not `gh`, and are
out of scope. Only the `gh` *CLI* and GitHub-issue *concepts* leave Bram.

Phasing (each becomes its own child worklist item on approval — this
umbrella item is the plan of record, not a single big-bang apply):

1. **Backend** — replace `gh_*` fns and the `/__issue*` routes in
   `lib.rs` with `entire trail` invocations; update the
   `.claude/settings.json` allowlist.
2. **UI** — rename the Issues tab to **Trails**
   (`app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` → a Trails component) and rebind
   to the new routes; port the new-issue flow to trail create.
3. **Close-on-commit** — swap `closesIssues` for a trail-status
   transition in `Workspace.xmlui` and the commit protocol.
4. **Docs** — rewrite the close-on-commit, `closesIssues`, and
   "Updating GitHub issues via gh" sections in `CLAUDE.md`,
   `conventions.md`, and `docs/apis.md`.

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang single apply across all files** — rejected: too large to
  review or roll back as one diff; the phases are independently testable.
- **Keep `gh` for read (Issues tab) and add trails alongside** —
  rejected: contradicts "no more GitHub"; leaves two issue systems.
- **[chosen] One umbrella plan item + phased child items** — documents
  the full mapping up front, lands in reviewable slices, each phase
  independently rejectable.

Open questions to resolve before/while applying (flagging, not blocking):

- `entire trail show` exposes no `--json` flag today (only `list` does).
  If `show` can't emit JSON, `/__issue`-equivalent detail view needs
  either a `list --json` + client join or an upstream `entire` ask.
- `entire trail list --author me` itself "requires gh CLI" per its help.
  Removing `gh` from the host may break the `me` author filter; either
  drop that filter or accept that `entire` internally may still use `gh`.
- Trails are branch-scoped; today's Issues tab is repo-global. Confirm the
  Trails tab should list `--status any` across branches, not just the
  current branch's trail.

- **Close-on-commit dialog** — `app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui`
  drives `closesIssues`, the confirm dialog, and the
  `close-issue:` / `push-before-close:` feedback protocol.
- **Allowlist** — `.claude/settings.json` permits the `/__issue*`
  loopback curl.
- **Conventions** — `CLAUDE.md` and `app/__shell/conventions.md` document
  `closesIssues`, the close-on-commit dialog, "Updating GitHub issues via
  gh", and the `gh issue`-only work carve-out. `docs/apis.md` documents
  the `/__issue*` routes.

# After

Remove all `gh`-CLI usage from Bram and replace the GitHub-issue concept
with **Entire trails** (`entire trail …`), a branch-scoped context object
with `create` / `show` / `list` / `update` / `delete` / `finding` /
`watch` subcommands. The app makes zero `gh` calls; trail work goes
through `entire trail` (which itself addresses a repo as
`--repo gh/owner/repo`, so GitHub can remain the underlying *forge*
without Bram ever invoking `gh`).

Concept mapping:

| Today (`gh`) | Replacement (`entire trail`) |
|---|---|
| `gh issue list` → `/__issues` | `entire trail list --status any --json` |
| `gh issue view N` → `/__issue` | `entire trail show <n> --json` |
| `gh issue list --search q` → `/__issues/search` | `entire trail list --json` + local filter |
| `gh issue comment` → `/__issue/comment` | `entire trail finding add` / `entire trail update` |
| `gh issue close` → `/__issue/close` | `entire trail update --status closed` (+ `finding resolve`) |
| `closesIssues` / close-on-commit dialog | `resolvesTrails` — `entire trail update --status merged/closed` after commit |
| `gh api` commit-visible verify | trail status update is local-first; drop the GitHub round-trip |

**Scope boundary:** `git` itself stays — commit and push (Commits-tab
Push button, worklist-commit route) are git operations, not `gh`, and are
out of scope. Only the `gh` *CLI* and GitHub-issue *concepts* leave Bram.

Phasing (each becomes its own child worklist item on approval — this
umbrella item is the plan of record, not a single big-bang apply):

1. **Backend** — replace `gh_*` fns and the `/__issue*` routes in
   `lib.rs` with `entire trail` invocations; update the
   `.claude/settings.json` allowlist.
2. **UI** — rename the Issues tab to **Trails**
   (`app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` → a Trails component) and rebind
   to the new routes; port the new-issue flow to trail create.
3. **Close-on-commit** — swap `closesIssues` for a trail-status
   transition in `Workspace.xmlui` and the commit protocol.
4. **Docs** — rewrite the close-on-commit, `closesIssues`, and
   "Updating GitHub issues via gh" sections in `CLAUDE.md`,
   `conventions.md`, and `docs/apis.md`.

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang single apply across all files** — rejected: too large to
  review or roll back as one diff; the phases are independently testable.
- **Keep `gh` for read (Issues tab) and add trails alongside** —
  rejected: contradicts "no more GitHub"; leaves two issue systems.
- **[chosen] One umbrella plan item + phased child items** — documents
  the full mapping up front, lands in reviewable slices, each phase
  independently rejectable.

Open questions to resolve before/while applying (flagging, not blocking):

- `entire trail show` exposes no `--json` flag today (only `list` does).
  If `show` can't emit JSON, `/__issue`-equivalent detail view needs
  either a `list --json` + client join or an upstream `entire` ask.
- `entire trail list --author me` itself "requires gh CLI" per its help.
  Removing `gh` from the host may break the `me` author filter; either
  drop that filter or accept that `entire` internally may still use `gh`.
- Trails are branch-scoped; today's Issues tab is repo-global. Confirm the
  Trails tab should list `--status any` across branches, not just the
  current branch's trail.

content.text:
Proposed **`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails`** — it's in the Worklist tab now as TO APPLY.

The gist of what I found and proposed:

- **Every issue-shaped feature in Bram shells out to `gh`.** In `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`: the `/__issues`, `/__issue`, `/__issues/search`, `/__issue/comment`, `/__issue/close`, `/__repo/origin` routes (backed by `gh_issues_list`, `gh_issue_view`, `gh_issue_close`, `gh_commit_visible`, etc.); the Issues tab (`Issues.xmlui`); close-on-commit in `Workspace.xmlui`; the `closesIssues` protocol in `CLAUDE.md`/`conventions.md`; and the `/__issue*` allowlist entry.
- **`entire trail` maps cleanly onto issues** — it's a hidden/labs but real command with `create`/`show`/`list`/`update`/`delete`/`finding`/`watch`. The draft has a full concept-mapping table.

Two things I want to flag before you approve:

1. **This is deliberately an umbrella/plan item, not a single apply.** The `files` list spans the whole surface, but the prose lays out 4 phased child items (backend → UI → close-on-commit → docs). I recommend it lands in slices, not one big-bang diff. If you'd rather I file it as separate items up front, Iterate with that direction.
2. **Two real wrinkles in `entire`:** `entire trail show` has no `--json` flag today (only `list` does), and `entire trail list --author me` *itself requires the `gh` CLI* per its help. So "no GitHub in the app" is achievable, but `entire` may still lean on `gh` internally for some paths. Details are in the draft's "Open questions".

Approve to start, or Iterate to redirect scope/phasing.

message.content: 1. Up to you, if slices or separate items comes more naturally with the Bram model whatever is fine. This is also me learning how to use Bram 2. No —json flag, ok? Why is that a problem. As for it using the `gh` cli under the hood: that’s fine

content.text:
Good — let me answer the `--json` question, then reshape the proposal to fit how Bram wants you to work.

**Why the missing `--json` matters (and why it's minor):** the Rust routes don't render UI — they hand structured data to the Trails/Issues tab's `DataSource`, which the XMLUI binds to fields like `.title`, `.body`, `.status`. `gh` gives us clean JSON to deserialize. `entire trail list` has `--json`, so the *list* view is fine. But `entire trail show` (the single-trail detail pane behind `/__issue`) only prints human terminal text today. Parsing that text is fragile — column widths, colors, wrapping all shift. So the "problem" is just: **how do we get structured detail for one trail?** Three options, none blocking:
- join the detail from `trail list --json` (works if `list` carries body/findings),
- scrape `trail show` text (fragile — avoid),
- ask Entire to add `show --json` upstream.

Since you're fine with `gh`-under-the-hood, that open question drops entirely.

**On slices vs. items — the Bram-native answer:** a worklist item is the unit of *one approve → one commit*. Four phases that each want their own review and commit are four items. But phases 2–4's exact edits depend on how phase 1 lands, so proposing them now would be speculative. The natural Bram rhythm is: **propose the concrete near-term slice, land it, then propose the next.** So I'll scope this item to **Phase 1 (backend routes + allowlist)** and keep the full plan in the draft as the map; phases 2–4 get proposed as their own items once phase 1 commits.

Let me reshape the item to match that.

**This item = Phase 1 only** (backend routes + allowlist). The rest of
the migration is mapped below as the plan of record; each later phase is
proposed as its own worklist item once its predecessor commits, because
its exact edits depend on how Phase 1 lands.

/ `delete` / `finding` / `watch` subcommands. The app makes zero `gh`

`src-tauri/src/lib.rs` + `.claude/settings.json`:

- Replace the `gh_issues_list` / `gh_issues_search` / `gh_issue_view`
  helpers behind `/__issues`, `/__issue`, `/__issues/search` with

- Drop the `gh api` commit-visible round-trip (`gh_commit_visible`);
  trail status is local-first.

  any `entire` calls are permitted; remove `gh`-specific entries.

renames it.

## Phase 1 (this item's apply)

`src-tauri/src/lib.rs` + `.claude/settings.json`:

- Replace the `gh_issues_list` / `gh_issues_search` / `gh_issue_view`
  helpers behind `/__issues`, `/__issue`, `/__issues/search` with
  `entire trail list --status any --json` and per-trail detail (see
  detail-JSON note below).
- Replace `gh_issue_comment` / `gh_issue_close` behind
  `/__issue/comment` and `/__issue/close` with
  `entire trail finding add` and `entire trail update --status …`.
- Drop the `gh api` commit-visible round-trip (`gh_commit_visible`);
  trail status is local-first.
- Swap the `.claude/settings.json` allowlist so the loopback curl and
  any `entire` calls are permitted; remove `gh`-specific entries.

The Issues-tab UI keeps binding the same route names in Phase 1 (routes
change payload source, not shape), so the tab keeps working until Phase 2
renames it.

Concept mapping:

| Today (`gh`) | Replacement (`entire trail`) |
|---|---|
| `gh issue list` → `/__issues` | `entire trail list --status any --json` |
| `gh issue view N` → `/__issue` | `entire trail show <n> --json` |
| `gh issue list --search q` → `/__issues/search` | `entire trail list --json` + local filter |
| `gh issue comment` → `/__issue/comment` | `entire trail finding add` / `entire trail update` |
| `gh issue close` → `/__issue/close` | `entire trail update --status closed` (+ `finding resolve`) |
| `closesIssues` / close-on-commit dialog | `resolvesTrails` — `entire trail update --status merged/closed` after commit |
| `gh api` commit-visible verify | trail status update is local-first; drop the GitHub round-trip |

**Scope boundary:** `git` itself stays — commit and push (Commits-tab
Push button, worklist-commit route) are git operations, not `gh`, and are
out of scope. Only the `gh` *CLI* and GitHub-issue *concepts* leave Bram.

Phasing (each becomes its own child worklist item on approval — this
umbrella item is the plan of record, not a single big-bang apply):

1. **Backend** — replace `gh_*` fns and the `/__issue*` routes in
   `lib.rs` with `entire trail` invocations; update the
   `.claude/settings.json` allowlist.
2. **UI** — rename the Issues tab to **Trails**
   (`app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` → a Trails component) and rebind
   to the new routes; port the new-issue flow to trail create.
3. **Close-on-commit** — swap `closesIssues` for a trail-status
   transition in `Workspace.xmlui` and the commit protocol.
4. **Docs** — rewrite the close-on-commit, `closesIssues`, and
   "Updating GitHub issues via gh" sections in `CLAUDE.md`,
   `conventions.md`, and `docs/apis.md`.

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang single apply across all files** — rejected: too large to
  review or roll back as one diff; the phases are independently testable.
- **Keep `gh` for read (Issues tab) and add trails alongside** —
  rejected: contradicts "no more GitHub"; leaves two issue systems.
- **[chosen] One umbrella plan item + phased child items** — documents
  the full mapping up front, lands in reviewable slices, each phase
  independently rejectable.

Open questions to resolve before/while applying (flagging, not blocking):

- `entire trail show` exposes no `--json` flag today (only `list` does).
  If `show` can't emit JSON, `/__issue`-equivalent detail view needs
  either a `list --json` + client join or an upstream `entire` ask.
- `entire trail list --author me` itself "requires gh CLI" per its help.
  Removing `gh` from the host may break the `me` author filter; either
  drop that filter or accept that `entire` internally may still use `gh`.
- Trails are branch-scoped; today's Issues tab is repo-global. Confirm the
  Trails tab should list `--status any` across branches, not just the
  current branch's trail.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Before

Bram is coupled to GitHub through the `gh` CLI. Everything issue-shaped
in the app shells out to `gh`, and the agent-facing conventions are
written around GitHub issues.

Runtime touchpoints (all in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` unless noted):

- **Issue routes** — `/__issues` (list), `/__issue` (view), `/__issues/search`,
  `/__issue/comment`, `/__issue/close`, `/__repo/origin`. Backed by
  `gh_issues_list`, `gh_issues_search`, `gh_issue_view`,
  `gh_issue_comment`, `gh_issue_close`, `gh_issue_cross_references`,
  `gh_issue_closed_event_actor`, `enrich_issue_activity`.
- **Close-on-commit** — `gh_commit_visible` (`gh api` commit lookup),
  `gh_commit_missing_stderr`, `gh_issue_close_with_commit`,
  `handle_issue_close_json`, `close_issue_commit_comment`,
  `parse_github_commit_url`, plus the `gh api /user` login used to
  attribute commits.
- **Issues tab UI** — `app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` binds the six
  routes above and renders the whole issue browser/search/new-issue flow.
- **Close-on-commit dialog** — `app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui`
  drives `closesIssues`, the confirm dialog, and the
  `close-issue:` / `push-before-close:` feedback protocol.
- **Allowlist** — `.claude/settings.json` permits the `/__issue*`
  loopback curl.
- **Conventions** — `CLAUDE.md` and `app/__shell/conventions.md` document
  `closesIssues`, the close-on-commit dialog, "Updating GitHub issues via
  gh", and the `gh issue`-only work carve-out. `docs/apis.md` documents
  the `/__issue*` routes.

# After

**This item = Phase 1 only** (backend routes + allowlist). The rest of
the migration is mapped below as the plan of record; each later phase is
proposed as its own worklist item once its predecessor commits, because
its exact edits depend on how Phase 1 lands.

The end state: remove all `gh`-CLI usage from Bram and replace the
GitHub-issue concept with **Entire trails** (`entire trail …`), a
branch-scoped context object with `create` / `show` / `list` / `update`
/ `delete` / `finding` / `watch` subcommands. The app makes zero `gh`
calls; trail work goes through `entire trail` (which itself addresses a
repo as `--repo gh/owner/repo` and may use `gh` internally — that's fine,
the goal is Bram never invoking `gh` directly).

## Phase 1 (this item's apply)

`src-tauri/src/lib.rs` + `.claude/settings.json`:

- Replace the `gh_issues_list` / `gh_issues_search` / `gh_issue_view`
  helpers behind `/__issues`, `/__issue`, `/__issues/search` with
  `entire trail list --status any --json` and per-trail detail (see
  detail-JSON note below).
- Replace `gh_issue_comment` / `gh_issue_close` behind
  `/__issue/comment` and `/__issue/close` with
  `entire trail finding add` and `entire trail update --status …`.
- Drop the `gh api` commit-visible round-trip (`gh_commit_visible`);
  trail status is local-first.
- Swap the `.claude/settings.json` allowlist so the loopback curl and
  any `entire` calls are permitted; remove `gh`-specific entries.

The Issues-tab UI keeps binding the same route names in Phase 1 (routes
change payload source, not shape), so the tab keeps working until Phase 2
renames it.

Concept mapping:

| Today (`gh`) | Replacement (`entire trail`) |
|---|---|
| `gh issue list` → `/__issues` | `entire trail list --status any --json` |
| `gh issue view N` → `/__issue` | `entire trail show <n> --json` |
| `gh issue list --search q` → `/__issues/search` | `entire trail list --json` + local filter |
| `gh issue comment` → `/__issue/comment` | `entire trail finding add` / `entire trail update` |
| `gh issue close` → `/__issue/close` | `entire trail update --status closed` (+ `finding resolve`) |
| `closesIssues` / close-on-commit dialog | `resolvesTrails` — `entire trail update --status merged/closed` after commit |
| `gh api` commit-visible verify | trail status update is local-first; drop the GitHub round-trip |

**Scope boundary:** `git` itself stays — commit and push (Commits-tab
Push button, worklist-commit route) are git operations, not `gh`, and are
out of scope. Only the `gh` *CLI* and GitHub-issue *concepts* leave Bram.

## Follow-up phases (proposed as their own items after Phase 1 commits)

2. **UI** — rename the Issues tab to **Trails**
   (`app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` → a Trails component) and rebind;
   port the new-issue flow to trail create.
3. **Close-on-commit** — swap `closesIssues` for a trail-status
   transition in `Workspace.xmlui` and the commit protocol.
4. **Docs** — rewrite the close-on-commit, `closesIssues`, and
   "Updating GitHub issues via gh" sections in `CLAUDE.md`,
   `conventions.md`, and `docs/apis.md`.

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang single apply across all files** — rejected: too large to
  review or roll back as one diff; the phases are independently testable.
- **Keep `gh` for read (Issues tab) and add trails alongside** —
  rejected: contradicts "no more GitHub"; leaves two issue systems.
- **[chosen] One umbrella plan item + phased child items** — documents
  the full mapping up front, lands in reviewable slices, each phase
  independently rejectable.

Open questions to resolve before/while applying (flagging, not blocking):

- `entire trail show` exposes no `--json` flag today (only `list` does).
  If `show` can't emit JSON, `/__issue`-equivalent detail view needs
  either a `list --json` + client join or an upstream `entire` ask.
- `entire trail list --author me` itself "requires gh CLI" per its help.
  Removing `gh` from the host may break the `me` author filter; either
  drop that filter or accept that `entire` internally may still use `gh`.
- Trails are branch-scoped; today's Issues tab is repo-global. Confirm the
  Trails tab should list `--status any` across branches, not just the
  current branch's trail.

content.text:
Reshaped. The item is now scoped to **Phase 1** (`src-tauri/src/lib.rs` + `.claude/settings.json`) — the backend route swap — with the full four-phase plan kept in the draft as the map. Both your answers are folded in: the `--json` detail question resolves to "join from `trail list --json`, don't text-scrape," and `gh`-under-the-hood is now recorded as accepted.

What this teaches about the Bram model: the Worklist tab's inline diff will show exactly the `lib.rs` + `settings.json` change when you're ready to commit — a reviewable slice, not a 7-file blob. Approve Phase 1, we land it, and I propose Phase 2 (rename Issues tab → Trails) as a fresh item against the real Phase 1 code.

Approve when ready, or Iterate if you want the backend scope adjusted.

input.content:
# Before

Bram hand-rolls its own view of agent work across three tabs, each
backed by host routes in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` that parse provider
session JSONL (Claude Code / Codex transcripts) directly:

- **Sessions tab** (`app/tools/components/Sessions.xmlui`) —
  `/__sessions/list`, `/__sessions/meta`, `/__sessions/search`,
  `/__sessions/rename`, `/__sessions/delete`, `/__turns`. Bram walks the
  transcript files itself to derive session lists, per-turn history, and
  token counts.
- **Context tab** (`app/tools/components/Context.xmlui`) —
  `/__context/list`, `/__context/file`, `/__context/search`. Views
  CLAUDE.md + memory + hooks + settings.
- **History tab** (`app/tools/components/History.xmlui`) —
  `/__worklist-history/list`, `/__worklist-history/search`. Bram's own
  worklist lifecycle audit.

`entire` already tracks this locally. `entire session list --json`
returns, per active/recent session: `session_id`, `agent`, `model`,
`status`, `branch`, `worktree_path`, `started_at`, `last_active`,
`turns`, `checkpoints`, `tokens{total,input,cache_read,cache_write,
output}`, `last_prompt`, `files_touched`. `entire session info
<id> --json` gives detail; `entire checkpoint list [--session <id>]`
lists checkpoints (agent-work records tied to commits); `entire
checkpoint explain` and `entire checkpoint search` (semantic + keyword)
cover per-checkpoint narrative and cross-session search.

# After

**Yes — partially, and the good parts are local-first.** Unlike the
trails migration (cloud-gated, 404s until the repo is registered),
`entire session`/`entire checkpoint` return real JSON on this machine
today, so this is verifiable end-to-end without any backend setup.

What fits, what doesn't:

- **Sessions tab → strong fit.** `entire session list --json` /
  `session info --json` already carry everything the tab derives by
  hand (list, model, branch, turns, tokens, last prompt, files touched).
  Replacing the JSONL-walking routes with `entire` calls deletes a lot
  of fragile parsing.
- **Turn/checkpoint history → good fit.** `entire checkpoint list
  --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain` map onto `/__turns`-style
  per-session history, with `checkpoint search` adding semantic search
  Bram doesn't have.
- **History tab (worklist lifecycle) → out of scope.** This is Bram's
  own proposed→applied→committed audit, not agent-work history. `entire`
  has no concept of it. Leave as-is.
- **Context tab → out of scope.** CLAUDE.md / memory / hooks / settings
  are not sessions or checkpoints. Leave as-is.

**This item = Phase 1 only:** swap `/__sessions/list` (and its `/meta`
enrichment) in `lib.rs` to shell out to `entire session list --json`,
and rebind `Sessions.xmlui` to the resulting fields. It's the smallest
independently-verifiable slice and proves the field mapping against live
local output before touching search/turns/delete.

Follow-up phases (proposed as their own items after Phase 1 commits):

2. **Per-session turns/checkpoints** — `/__turns` → `entire checkpoint
   list --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain`.
3. **Search** — `/__sessions/search` → `entire checkpoint search`
   (semantic + keyword).
4. **Rename/delete** — reconcile Bram's `/__sessions/rename` +
   `/delete` with `entire session` / `entire clean` semantics (open
   question: `entire` may not support rename; may need to stay Bram-side).

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang: replace all six session routes at once** — rejected: the
  rename/delete semantics don't obviously map to `entire`, so bundling
  them risks blocking the parts that clearly do.
- **Keep Bram's JSONL parsing, add `entire` as a second source** —
  rejected: two derivations of the same truth drift; the point is to
  delete the hand-rolled parser.
- **[chosen] Phase 1 = Sessions list swap**, verify field mapping on
  live local data, then expand.

Sequencing note: Phase 1 touches `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`, which the
`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` item also touches. That item is
still TO APPLY (its apply is paused — see chat), so there's no on-disk
conflict yet, but whichever lands first, the second should rebase its
`lib.rs` edits on the first to avoid a tangled diff.

Open question to confirm before applying:

- Bram's Sessions tab lists sessions across worktrees/branches for the
  project; `entire session list` is global across all tracked worktrees.
  Confirm the tab should show `entire`'s full list (with a project
  filter) rather than only this project's sessions.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Before

Bram is coupled to GitHub through the `gh` CLI. Everything issue-shaped
in the app shells out to `gh`, and the agent-facing conventions are
written around GitHub issues.

Runtime touchpoints (all in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` unless noted):

- **Issue routes** — `/__issues` (list), `/__issue` (view), `/__issues/search`,
  `/__issue/comment`, `/__issue/close`, `/__repo/origin`. Backed by
  `gh_issues_list`, `gh_issues_search`, `gh_issue_view`,
  `gh_issue_comment`, `gh_issue_close`, `gh_issue_cross_references`,
  `gh_issue_closed_event_actor`, `enrich_issue_activity`.
- **Close-on-commit** — `gh_commit_visible` (`gh api` commit lookup),
  `gh_commit_missing_stderr`, `gh_issue_close_with_commit`,
  `handle_issue_close_json`, `close_issue_commit_comment`,
  `parse_github_commit_url`, plus the `gh api /user` login used to
  attribute commits.
- **Issues tab UI** — `app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` binds the six
  routes above and renders the whole issue browser/search/new-issue flow.
- **Close-on-commit dialog** — `app/tools/components/Workspace.xmlui`
  drives `closesIssues`, the confirm dialog, and the
  `close-issue:` / `push-before-close:` feedback protocol.
- **Allowlist** — `.claude/settings.json` permits the `/__issue*`
  loopback curl.
- **Conventions** — `CLAUDE.md` and `app/__shell/conventions.md` document
  `closesIssues`, the close-on-commit dialog, "Updating GitHub issues via
  gh", and the `gh issue`-only work carve-out. `docs/apis.md` documents
  the `/__issue*` routes.

# After

**This item = Phase 1 only** (backend routes + allowlist). The rest of
the migration is mapped below as the plan of record; each later phase is
proposed as its own worklist item once its predecessor commits, because
its exact edits depend on how Phase 1 lands.

The end state: remove all `gh`-CLI usage from Bram and replace the
GitHub-issue concept with **Entire trails** (`entire trail …`), a
branch-scoped context object with `create` / `show` / `list` / `update`
/ `delete` / `finding` / `watch` subcommands. The app makes zero `gh`
calls; trail work goes through `entire trail` (which itself addresses a
repo as `--repo gh/owner/repo` and may use `gh` internally — that's fine,
the goal is Bram never invoking `gh` directly).

## Phase 1 (this item's apply)

`src-tauri/src/lib.rs` + `.claude/settings.json`:

- Replace the `gh_issues_list` / `gh_issues_search` / `gh_issue_view`
  helpers behind `/__issues`, `/__issue`, `/__issues/search` with
  `entire trail list --status any --json` and per-trail detail (see
  detail-JSON note below).
- Replace `gh_issue_comment` / `gh_issue_close` behind
  `/__issue/comment` and `/__issue/close` with
  `entire trail finding add` and `entire trail update --status …`.
- Drop the `gh api` commit-visible round-trip (`gh_commit_visible`);
  trail status is local-first.
- Swap the `.claude/settings.json` allowlist so the loopback curl and
  any `entire` calls are permitted; remove `gh`-specific entries.

The Issues-tab UI keeps binding the same route names in Phase 1 (routes
change payload source, not shape), so the tab keeps working until Phase 2
renames it.

Concept mapping:

| Today (`gh`) | Replacement (`entire trail`) |
|---|---|
| `gh issue list` → `/__issues` | `entire trail list --status any --json` |
| `gh issue view N` → `/__issue` | `entire trail show <n> --json` |
| `gh issue list --search q` → `/__issues/search` | `entire trail list --json` + local filter |
| `gh issue comment` → `/__issue/comment` | `entire trail finding add` / `entire trail update` |
| `gh issue close` → `/__issue/close` | `entire trail update --status closed` (+ `finding resolve`) |
| `closesIssues` / close-on-commit dialog | `resolvesTrails` — `entire trail update --status merged/closed` after commit |
| `gh api` commit-visible verify | trail status update is local-first; drop the GitHub round-trip |

**Scope boundary:** `git` itself stays — commit and push (Commits-tab
Push button, worklist-commit route) are git operations, not `gh`, and are
out of scope. Only the `gh` *CLI* and GitHub-issue *concepts* leave Bram.

## Follow-up phases (proposed as their own items after Phase 1 commits)

2. **UI** — rename the Issues tab to **Trails**
   (`app/tools/components/Issues.xmlui` → a Trails component) and rebind;
   port the new-issue flow to trail create.
3. **Close-on-commit** — swap `closesIssues` for a trail-status
   transition in `Workspace.xmlui` and the commit protocol.
4. **Docs** — rewrite the close-on-commit, `closesIssues`, and
   "Updating GitHub issues via gh" sections in `CLAUDE.md`,
   `conventions.md`, and `docs/apis.md`.

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang single apply across all files** — rejected: too large to
  review or roll back as one diff; the phases are independently testable.
- **Keep `gh` for read (Issues tab) and add trails alongside** —
  rejected: contradicts "no more GitHub"; leaves two issue systems.
- **[chosen] One umbrella plan item + phased child items** — documents
  the full mapping up front, lands in reviewable slices, each phase
  independently rejectable.

Notes (resolved with the user):

- **Detail JSON** — `entire trail show` has no `--json` flag today (only
  `list` does). The routes deserialize structured data for the UI, so
  Phase 1's per-trail detail joins fields from `trail list --json` rather
  than scraping `trail show` text. If `list --json` lacks body/findings,
  fall back to an upstream `show --json` ask; do not text-scrape.
- **`gh` under the hood is fine** — `entire trail list --author me` and
  similar may call `gh` internally. Accepted: the goal is that *Bram*
  never invokes `gh` directly, not that `gh` is absent from the host.

Still to confirm during Phase 2:

- Trails are branch-scoped; today's Issues tab is repo-global. Confirm the
  Trails tab lists `--status any` across branches, not just the current
  branch's trail.

content.text: That's the crux of the feasibility question, and I now have the answer. The key file is `read_session_turns` / the `/__turns` projection (`lib.rs:14581`): Bram's Sessions view isn't just a session list — it's a **fine-grained conversation projection** that reads raw provider JSONL and resolves Bram-specific artifacts (outbound-turn envelopes, message/feedback draft refs, image markers, Codex fanout dedupe). `entire` exposes nothing like raw turn content. Let me record the finding in the draft and report.

input.old_string:
# After

**Yes — partially, and the good parts are local-first.** Unlike the
trails migration (cloud-gated, 404s until the repo is registered),
`entire session`/`entire checkpoint` return real JSON on this machine
today, so this is verifiable end-to-end without any backend setup.

What fits, what doesn't:

- **Sessions tab → strong fit.** `entire session list --json` /
  `session info --json` already carry everything the tab derives by
  hand (list, model, branch, turns, tokens, last prompt, files touched).
  Replacing the JSONL-walking routes with `entire` calls deletes a lot
  of fragile parsing.
- **Turn/checkpoint history → good fit.** `entire checkpoint list
  --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain` map onto `/__turns`-style
  per-session history, with `checkpoint search` adding semantic search
  Bram doesn't have.
- **History tab (worklist lifecycle) → out of scope.** This is Bram's
  own proposed→applied→committed audit, not agent-work history. `entire`
  has no concept of it. Leave as-is.
- **Context tab → out of scope.** CLAUDE.md / memory / hooks / settings
  are not sessions or checkpoints. Leave as-is.

**This item = Phase 1 only:** swap `/__sessions/list` (and its `/meta`
enrichment) in `lib.rs` to shell out to `entire session list --json`,
and rebind `Sessions.xmlui` to the resulting fields. It's the smallest
independently-verifiable slice and proves the field mapping against live
local output before touching search/turns/delete.

Follow-up phases (proposed as their own items after Phase 1 commits):

2. **Per-session turns/checkpoints** — `/__turns` → `entire checkpoint
   list --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain`.
3. **Search** — `/__sessions/search` → `entire checkpoint search`
   (semantic + keyword).
4. **Rename/delete** — reconcile Bram's `/__sessions/rename` +
   `/delete` with `entire session` / `entire clean` semantics (open
   question: `entire` may not support rename; may need to stay Bram-side).

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang: replace all six session routes at once** — rejected: the
  rename/delete semantics don't obviously map to `entire`, so bundling
  them risks blocking the parts that clearly do.
- **Keep Bram's JSONL parsing, add `entire` as a second source** —
  rejected: two derivations of the same truth drift; the point is to
  delete the hand-rolled parser.
- **[chosen] Phase 1 = Sessions list swap**, verify field mapping on
  live local data, then expand.

Sequencing note: Phase 1 touches `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`, which the
`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` item also touches. That item is
still TO APPLY (its apply is paused — see chat), so there's no on-disk
conflict yet, but whichever lands first, the second should rebase its
`lib.rs` edits on the first to avoid a tangled diff.

Open question to confirm before applying:

- Bram's Sessions tab lists sessions across worktrees/branches for the
  project; `entire session list` is global across all tracked worktrees.
  Confirm the tab should show `entire`'s full list (with a project
  filter) rather than only this project's sessions.
input.new_string:
# After

## Feasibility finding (2026-07-04)

**Partially feasible. They are the same concept at the *session-metadata*
level and a different concept at the *turn/transcript* level.** Verdict
per surface:

- **Session list metadata → clean swap (feasible).** `entire session
  list --json` / `session info --json` already carry everything the
  Sessions tab's list derives by hand — and more: `model`, `branch`,
  `status`, `turns`, `checkpoints`, full `tokens{...}` breakdown,
  `last_prompt`, `files_touched`. This is local-first and returns real
  JSON here today. Swapping `/__sessions/list` + `/__sessions/meta` to
  `entire session` deletes fragile parsing with net-positive fields.

- **Per-turn transcript → NOT a conceptual match (stays Bram-native).**
  Bram's `/__turns` (`read_session_turns`, `lib.rs:14581`) is a
  conversation *projection*: it reads raw provider JSONL and resolves
  Bram-only artifacts — outbound-turn envelopes, message-draft and
  feedback-draft refs, image markers, Codex storage-fanout dedupe.
  `entire` exposes no raw turn content at all — only counts
  (`turns: N`), commit-tied `checkpoint` records, `checkpoint explain`
  (AI summaries), and `checkpoint search`. An Entire "checkpoint" is a
  git-anchored work record, not a chat turn. So the transcript view
  cannot be sourced from `entire`; it must keep reading provider JSONL.

- **Search → additive, not a replacement.** `entire checkpoint search`
  (semantic + keyword) is a capability Bram lacks, but it searches
  checkpoints/commits, not the raw transcript that `/__sessions/search`
  greps. Worth adding as a *new* affordance later; not a swap.

- **Coverage tradeoff.** Bram reads provider JSONL directly, so it sees
  *every* Claude/Codex session for the project — including ones predating
  `entire enable` and ones in projects where Entire isn't installed.
  `entire session list` only knows sessions Entire tracked. Swapping the
  list means losing untracked/historical sessions unless we union both
  sources.

- **History tab (worklist lifecycle) & Context tab (CLAUDE.md / memory /
  hooks / settings) → out of scope.** Neither is a session or checkpoint
  concept; `entire` has no equivalent. Leave as-is.

## Recommended scope, given the finding

**Narrow this item to the one clean win: the Sessions *list*.** Swap
`/__sessions/list` (+ `/__sessions/meta`) in `lib.rs` to
`entire session list --json` and rebind `Sessions.xmlui`'s list to the
richer fields — either replacing the JSONL scan or unioning it with the
JSONL-derived list to preserve untracked-session coverage (decision
below). Leave `/__turns`, `/__sessions/search`, `/rename`, `/delete`
exactly as they are; they are Bram-native and don't have `entire`
equivalents.

Explicitly dropped from the earlier plan by this finding:

- ~~Phase 2: `/__turns` → `entire checkpoint`~~ — not feasible; no raw
  turn content in `entire`.
- ~~Phase 3: `/__sessions/search` → `entire checkpoint search`~~ —
  different corpus; reclassify as a future additive feature, not a swap.

Alternatives considered:

- **Full replacement of the Sessions surface** — rejected by the
  finding: the transcript projection has no `entire` equivalent.
- **[chosen] Replace only the session *list* source**, keep transcript /
  search / rename / delete Bram-native.

Open decision before applying (needs the user):

- **Replace vs. union for the list.** Replace `/__sessions/list` with
  `entire session list` (simpler, richer fields, but loses
  untracked/historical sessions), or union `entire`'s list with the
  existing JSONL scan (keeps full coverage, more code)? Recommend
  **union** so no session silently disappears from the tab.

Sequencing note: touches `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`, which the paused
`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` item also touches. No on-disk
conflict while that one is unapplied.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Before

Bram hand-rolls its own view of agent work across three tabs, each
backed by host routes in `src-tauri/src/lib.rs` that parse provider
session JSONL (Claude Code / Codex transcripts) directly:

- **Sessions tab** (`app/tools/components/Sessions.xmlui`) —
  `/__sessions/list`, `/__sessions/meta`, `/__sessions/search`,
  `/__sessions/rename`, `/__sessions/delete`, `/__turns`. Bram walks the
  transcript files itself to derive session lists, per-turn history, and
  token counts.
- **Context tab** (`app/tools/components/Context.xmlui`) —
  `/__context/list`, `/__context/file`, `/__context/search`. Views
  CLAUDE.md + memory + hooks + settings.
- **History tab** (`app/tools/components/History.xmlui`) —
  `/__worklist-history/list`, `/__worklist-history/search`. Bram's own
  worklist lifecycle audit.

`entire` already tracks this locally. `entire session list --json`
returns, per active/recent session: `session_id`, `agent`, `model`,
`status`, `branch`, `worktree_path`, `started_at`, `last_active`,
`turns`, `checkpoints`, `tokens{total,input,cache_read,cache_write,
output}`, `last_prompt`, `files_touched`. `entire session info
<id> --json` gives detail; `entire checkpoint list [--session <id>]`
lists checkpoints (agent-work records tied to commits); `entire
checkpoint explain` and `entire checkpoint search` (semantic + keyword)
cover per-checkpoint narrative and cross-session search.

# After

**Yes — partially, and the good parts are local-first.** Unlike the
trails migration (cloud-gated, 404s until the repo is registered),
`entire session`/`entire checkpoint` return real JSON on this machine
today, so this is verifiable end-to-end without any backend setup.

What fits, what doesn't:

- **Sessions tab → strong fit.** `entire session list --json` /
  `session info --json` already carry everything the tab derives by
  hand (list, model, branch, turns, tokens, last prompt, files touched).
  Replacing the JSONL-walking routes with `entire` calls deletes a lot
  of fragile parsing.
- **Turn/checkpoint history → good fit.** `entire checkpoint list
  --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain` map onto `/__turns`-style
  per-session history, with `checkpoint search` adding semantic search
  Bram doesn't have.
- **History tab (worklist lifecycle) → out of scope.** This is Bram's
  own proposed→applied→committed audit, not agent-work history. `entire`
  has no concept of it. Leave as-is.
- **Context tab → out of scope.** CLAUDE.md / memory / hooks / settings
  are not sessions or checkpoints. Leave as-is.

**This item = Phase 1 only:** swap `/__sessions/list` (and its `/meta`
enrichment) in `lib.rs` to shell out to `entire session list --json`,
and rebind `Sessions.xmlui` to the resulting fields. It's the smallest
independently-verifiable slice and proves the field mapping against live
local output before touching search/turns/delete.

Follow-up phases (proposed as their own items after Phase 1 commits):

2. **Per-session turns/checkpoints** — `/__turns` → `entire checkpoint
   list --session <id>` + `checkpoint explain`.
3. **Search** — `/__sessions/search` → `entire checkpoint search`
   (semantic + keyword).
4. **Rename/delete** — reconcile Bram's `/__sessions/rename` +
   `/delete` with `entire session` / `entire clean` semantics (open
   question: `entire` may not support rename; may need to stay Bram-side).

Alternatives considered:

- **Big-bang: replace all six session routes at once** — rejected: the
  rename/delete semantics don't obviously map to `entire`, so bundling
  them risks blocking the parts that clearly do.
- **Keep Bram's JSONL parsing, add `entire` as a second source** —
  rejected: two derivations of the same truth drift; the point is to
  delete the hand-rolled parser.
- **[chosen] Phase 1 = Sessions list swap**, verify field mapping on
  live local data, then expand.

Sequencing note: Phase 1 touches `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`, which the
`migrate-github-gh-to-entire-trails` item also touches. That item is
still TO APPLY (its apply is paused — see chat), so there's no on-disk
conflict yet, but whichever lands first, the second should rebase its
`lib.rs` edits on the first to avoid a tangled diff.

Open question to confirm before applying:

- Bram's Sessions tab lists sessions across worktrees/branches for the
  project; `entire session list` is global across all tracked worktrees.
  Confirm the tab should show `entire`'s full list (with a project
  filter) rather than only this project's sessions.

aiTitle: Migrate from gh CLI to entire CLI with trails

content.text:
Feasibility finding is in — I did **not** apply anything yet (you asked to figure out feasibility first, so I stopped at the finding and refined the item's draft to match). Verdict:

**Partially feasible — same concept for session *metadata*, different concept for *transcripts*.**

| Surface | Verdict |
|---|---|
| **Sessions list** (`/__sessions/list`, `/__sessions/meta`) | ✅ Clean swap. `entire session list --json` carries everything Bram derives by hand *plus more* (model, branch, token breakdown, checkpoints, `files_touched`). Works locally today. |
| **Transcript** (`/__turns`) | ❌ Not a match. `/__turns` is a conversation projection resolving Bram-only artifacts (outbound-turn envelopes, draft/feedback refs, image markers, Codex fanout). `entire` exposes **no raw turn content** — only counts, commit-tied checkpoints, and AI summaries. Stays Bram-native. |
| **Search** (`/__sessions/search`) | ➕ Different corpus. `entire checkpoint search` is semantic-over-checkpoints, not transcript grep. A *new* feature, not a swap. |
| **History tab / Context tab** | — Out of scope; no `entire` equivalent. |

The conceptual gap in one line: an `entire` **checkpoint is a git-anchored work record**, not a chat turn — great for "what work happened tied to which commit," useless as a transcript source.

**One coverage tradeoff to decide:** Bram reads provider JSONL directly, so it sees *every* Claude/Codex session — including ones from before `entire enable` or from non-Entire projects. `entire session list` only knows sessions it tracked. So swapping the list means:

- **Replace** — simpler, richer fields, but untracked/historical sessions vanish from the tab.
- **Union** (my recommendation) — merge `entire`'s list with the JSONL scan; no session ever silently disappears, at the cost of more code.

So the honest scope is: **narrow this item to just the Sessions-list source** (replace-or-union), and leave transcript/search/rename/delete alone. The draft now reflects exactly that.

Tell me **replace vs. union**, then re-approve the (narrowed) item in the Worklist tab and I'll implement + verify — or Iterate/Drop if you'd rather not bother for one list.

**On slices vs. items — the Bram-native answer:** a worklist item is the unit of *one approve → one commit*. Four phases that each want their own review and commit are four items. But phases 2–4's exact edits depend on how phase 1 lands, so proposing them now would be speculative. input.description: Check checkpoint explain json output

content.content:
=== checkpoint explain --help ===
Explain provides human-readable context about sessions, commits, and checkpoints.

Use this command to understand what happened during agent-driven development,
either for self-review or to understand a teammate's work.

By default, shows checkpoints on the current branch. Pass a checkpoint ID or
commit SHA as a positional argument to explain a specific item, or use flags.

Viewing specific items:
  entire explain <id-or-sha>           Auto-detects checkpoint ID or commit SHA
  entire explain --checkpoint <id>     Force interpretation as checkpoint ID
  entire explain --commit <ref>        Force interpretation as commit ref

Filtering the list view:
  --session      Filter checkpoints by session ID (or prefix)

Output verbosity levels (when explaining a specific item):
  Default:         Detailed view with scoped prompts (ID, session, tokens, intent, prompts, files)
  --short          Summary only (ID, session, timestamp, tokens, intent)
  --full           Parsed full transcript (all prompts/responses from entire session)
  --raw-transcript Raw transcript file (JSONL format)

Machine-readable export modes (additive surface for external consumers):
  --json           Metadata-only JSON. Lists checkpoints when no target is given;
                   emits a single checkpoint envelope when a target is supplied.
                   Transcript bytes are NEVER embedded in the JSON envelope.
  --transcript     Stream stored checkpoint transcript bytes (JSONL) to stdout
                   for the selected session. Same bytes as --raw-transcript
                   while checkpoints v1 is the checkpoint store.
  --session-index  Pick a session within a multi-session checkpoint (0-based).

=== exit 0 ===
